q: Why does an LLM output a probability distribution over the next word, rather than a single definitive answer?
ideal: Language is ambiguous - there are many plausible next words. A distribution lets the model weigh multiple likely options, then sample with some randomness (temperature).

q: What does 'tokenization' mean, and why doesn't the model just read whole words?
ideal: Tokens are sub-word chunks. Models share pieces across related words, handle novel words, and keep the vocabulary small enough to learn patterns efficiently.

q: AI can sound completely confident while being totally wrong. Why does this happen, and what should you do about it?
ideal: AI generates the most statistically likely response, not the factually correct one. It doesn't look up answers — it estimates them based on patterns in training data. Even when it sounds certain, you should verify important facts from a reliable source.
